meta.title: Capabilities in Xcind, Absent from Scind (Xcind-ahead)
meta.method: Five parallel subsystem subagents (CLI-surface, proxy/export, identity/isolation, hooks/generation, config/env) each enumerated Xcind's real as-built capability from bin/ + lib/xcind/*.bash and diffed it against Scind canon (/Users/beausimensen/Code/scind/docs) by topic. A synthesis pass de-duplicated cross-agent overlaps and applied the PROMOTE/DIVERGENCE/ESCALATE rule from global-context.md §2/§2a.
meta.operating_model: Scind is canon; Xcind's lessons upgrade the canon. DIVERGENCE must be EARNED (a one-sentence 'why Scind should NOT adopt'); when unsure, ESCALATE or PROMOTE, never silently diverge. Go/Bash language & build artifacts excluded (global-context §5).
meta.scope_notes: P4 covers Xcind-AHEAD capabilities only. Two reverse gaps (Xcind BEHIND Scind) surfaced by the proxy/export agent are recorded under reverse_gaps[] (hand to P5/P3), not as capability rows. The core XCIND_INSTANCE worktree-isolation token is NOT filed here: global-context §2a Example B already rules it a CANON-CHANGE against Scind ADR-0001, owned by P6/P3 (ADR-0019).
meta.supplement_2026_07_15: Human product-calls received after the initial synthesis, folded in (they OVERRIDE independent classification where they conflict; each affected row is tagged source='human product-call'): (1) Two-track docs / ADR-0014 -> PROMOTE (new row XA-0043). (2) XDG Base Directory split -> PROMOTE (new row XA-0044). (3) behaviors/ .feature files VERIFIED not test-exercised — see behaviors_verification below.

behaviors_verification.claim_checked: Are Xcind's engineering/behaviors/*.feature files exercised by any test target?
behaviors_verification.result: NO. `make test` runs three hand-written bash assertion scripts (test/test-xcind.sh, test/test-xcind-proxy.sh, test/test-xcind-prompt.sh; 9045 lines total). There is no cucumber/godog/behave/gherkin runner, and no test script, Makefile, or package.json references any .feature file. The 12 .feature files under engineering/behaviors/ are documentation-only Gherkin.
behaviors_verification.impact_on_this_artifact: No capability row here rests SOLELY on a .feature file — every behavior-derived row (XA-0018, XA-0021, XA-0022, XA-0023, XA-0028, XA-0040, XA-0041) also cites lib/xcind/ code and, in most cases, is covered by the bash test scripts. So the rows remain real; only the .feature citation is downgraded from 'test' to 'doc'.
behaviors_verification.human_intent: The human intends to DROP behaviors/ from BOTH projects. Treat .feature citations as design-doc evidence, not executable proof.

capabilities.capability: Generation-cache correctness for live-state content: XCIND_HOOKS_ALWAYS marks hooks whose overlay embeds live-allocated host ports / discovery values, so they re-run on an otherwise-valid (config-unchanged) cache hit while pure hooks replay from cache.
capabilities.why_it_exists: Assigned host ports and *_HOST_PORT discovery values are allocated from live host state, not from the config that keys the cache. Without an always-run carve-out, a config-unchanged cache hit would replay a stale overlay pointing at a moved allocation.
capabilities.rationale: A correctness principle, not a Bash mechanism: any cache keyed on configuration must treat content that embeds live host state (allocated ports, discovery vars) as non-cacheable and refresh it on hit. Applies equally to Scind's Go generator, whose manifest-vs-config staleness check does not distinguish config-derived from live-state-derived content.
capabilities.target_scind_change: In docs/specs/generated-manifest.md / state-management.md, specify that regeneration/staleness detection distinguishes config-derived override content (cacheable) from live-state-derived content (assigned host ports, discovery env vars) and mandates refreshing the latter even when the config-based staleness check reports 'up to date'.

capabilities.capability: Proxy domain must be multi-label (contain a dot); non-fatal startup warning when a single-label domain is used under a non-disabled TLS mode.
capabilities.why_it_exists: ADR-0009's promise that a wildcard cert covering *.${DOMAIN} makes TLS 'just work' fails empirically: a *.X wildcard is only honored by RFC 6125-strict TLS stacks (macOS LibreSSL curl/Safari, Go crypto/tls) when X has >=2 labels. A single-label domain (e.g. 'localhost') yields *.localhost, which strict matchers reject. Xcind added the constraint + warning.
capabilities.rationale: Scind's default domain (scind.test) happens to be multi-label so the bug is masked, but Scind's TLS spec never states the constraint or warns operators who set a single-label SCIND_PROXY_DOMAIN. The wildcard-matching rule is a protocol-level truth that generalizes to any implementation, Go included.
capabilities.target_scind_change: Add to docs/specs/proxy-infrastructure.md (TLS section) and/or ADR-0009 a constraint: the proxy domain MUST contain at least one dot, with a startup warning (not error) under a non-'disabled' TLS mode; cite the RFC 6125 strict-vs-lenient matching evidence from ADR-0016.

capabilities.capability: Assigned exports gain a _HOST_PORT service-discovery variable carrying the allocated host-published port, splitting in-network reachability (_HOST/_PORT) from host-side reachability (_HOST_PORT).
capabilities.why_it_exists: Scind pairs an assigned export's in-network _HOST with a single _PORT (the container-network port). Xcind adds _HOST_PORT so processes on the host (or via host.docker.internal) can discover the mapped host-published port; Scind's spec expressed only the in-network path.
capabilities.rationale: A genuine gap in Scind's env-var contract, strictly additive and forward-compatible: without _HOST_PORT a consumer cannot programmatically discover the host-facing port of an assigned service. ADR-0018 frames it as a 'divergence' but it is a learning, not a Bash-ism.
capabilities.target_scind_change: Add SCIND_{APP}_{SERVICE}_HOST_PORT (allocated host-published port) to the assigned-export variable set in docs/specs/environment-variables.md alongside the existing in-network _HOST/_PORT.

capabilities.capability: Configurable proxy host ports: XCIND_PROXY_HTTP_PORT / HTTPS_PORT / DASHBOARD_PORT (init flags + config) with the discovery _PORT/_URL correctly reflecting a non-default entrypoint port.
capabilities.why_it_exists: Scind hardcodes entrypoints at 80/443/8080 and exposes only TRAEFIK_IMAGE + config/state dirs. Xcind lets the operator remap published host ports (rootless dev, ports already taken) and threads the non-default port into the discovery URL builder so *_URL stays directly usable.
capabilities.rationale: Rootless/multi-instance dev where 80/443 are taken is a real need; the change is a clean superset (defaults unchanged). The non-trivial part Scind's spec omits is that discovery _PORT/_URL must reflect the custom port — Xcind demonstrates the correct coupling.
capabilities.target_scind_change: Add SCIND_PROXY_HTTP_PORT/HTTPS_PORT/DASHBOARD_PORT to proxy config (proxy.yaml + env), document that entrypoints publish those host ports, and specify that discovery _PORT/_URL include the port when non-default.

capabilities.capability: Per-export TLS metadata (tls=auto|require|disable) with a shared xcind-redirect-to-https middleware and noop@internal redirect-only HTTP routers.
capabilities.why_it_exists: Scind expresses redirect only at the entrypoint level ('web:80 redirects to HTTPS') with no per-export TLS knob. Xcind resolves an effective per-export mode and, for 'require', emits an HTTP router that hits a shared redirect middleware while pinning the redirect-only router's service to noop@internal (Traefik needn't resolve a backend for a request redirected before reaching one). The middleware is repeated on every rendered service block because Traefik's Docker provider loads labels only from running containers.
capabilities.rationale: Per-export redirect (vs blanket entrypoint redirect) lets one app mix HTTP-only and HTTPS-required exports. The two subtleties — noop@internal for redirect-only routers and emitting the middleware on every service block — are non-obvious correctness fixes. Scind's ADR-0009 is a stub that omits the tls attribute entirely.
capabilities.target_scind_change: Extend docs/specs/port-types.md and ADR-0009 with a per-export 'tls' attribute (auto/require/disable) and document the redirect-middleware pattern (shared middleware on every service block; redirect-only routers use noop@internal).

capabilities.capability: traefik.docker.network=<proxy-net> label + explicit dual-network attachment, and a pinned routers.{r}.service={r} binding.
capabilities.why_it_exists: Xcind attaches each exported service to both its default compose network and the shared proxy network. On a multi-network container, Traefik cannot guess which network to route on, so Xcind pins traefik.docker.network and explicitly binds each router to its own service. Scind's documented routing labels show neither.
capabilities.rationale: Any two-layer-networking design (Scind ADR-0002) puts service containers on >1 network — exactly the case where an omitted traefik.docker.network causes intermittent mis-routing. This is a correctness label Scind's docker-labels spec should mandate.
capabilities.target_scind_change: Add traefik.docker.network={proxy-network} to the required Traefik routing labels in docs/specs/docker-labels.md and document explicitly binding each router to its loadbalancer service.

capabilities.capability: Preferred scheme-agnostic .url label (xcind.export.{n}.url / xcind.apex.url) alongside per-protocol .http.url/.https.url, resolved to https when an HTTPS router serves the export else http.
capabilities.why_it_exists: Scind emits scind.export.{n}.proxy.{proto}.url per protocol. Xcind adds a preferred canonical xcind.export.{n}.url (and apex form) so consumers get a single 'the URL to use' without re-deriving scheme preference.
capabilities.rationale: The additive preferred-.url label is a clear convenience Scind lacks; consumers currently must encode the https-preferred rule themselves. (The label-schema shape difference — Xcind dropping the 'proxy.' segment — is a naming divergence, not promotable; see reverse_gaps.)
capabilities.target_scind_change: Add a canonical scind.export.{name}.url (and scind.apex.url) preferred-scheme label to docs/specs/docker-labels.md, defined as https when an HTTPS router serves the export else http.

capabilities.capability: TLS cert provisioning cascade: user-cert precedence > state-cache reuse > mkcert > openssl self-signed fallback (proper SAN/EKU/keyUsage, 600 key perms), plus a 'domain' marker file that regenerates the wildcard when XCIND_PROXY_DOMAIN changes.
capabilities.why_it_exists: Scind's proxy-infrastructure describes auto/custom/disabled at a high level ('falls back to self-signed') but leaves mechanics unspecified. Xcind implements a deterministic resolution order, an explicit openssl generator, and a sibling 'domain' marker so changing the domain invalidates and re-mints the wildcard instead of serving a stale-CN cert.
capabilities.rationale: The domain-change regeneration marker and the explicit fallback recipe close real operational gaps: without the marker, a domain change silently serves the wrong-CN cert. These are implementation contracts Scind's spec hand-waves — spec-level design, not Bash detail.
capabilities.target_scind_change: In docs/specs/proxy-infrastructure.md (TLS section), specify the auto-mode resolution order (user cert > cached > mkcert > openssl self-signed) and require regenerating the wildcard when the configured domain changes (e.g. via a recorded-domain marker).

capabilities.capability: Path-existence garbage collection of assigned ports (prune subcommand + auto-prune on init/up/status) plus a release PORT CLI.
capabilities.why_it_exists: Scind GCs via port_inventory status transitions and workspace/app removal. Xcind additionally prunes an assignment when its recorded app_path no longer exists on disk (a deleted app directory), and auto-prunes on every init/up/status so stale rows never accumulate.
capabilities.rationale: Filesystem-existence GC catches a case Scind's inventory model does not express: an app whose directory was deleted still holds its port reservation. Auto-pruning on read/write paths keeps state self-healing. Small, additive lifecycle rule.
capabilities.target_scind_change: Add to docs/specs/state-management.md a GC rule that drops an assigned-port entry whose recorded application path no longer exists, run on the port-inventory maintenance commands.

capabilities.capability: Context-aware shell prompt segment: the xcind-prompt binary (workspace/app + clickable apex URL via OSC 8, field-aware --detect availability probe, <500ms context-only fast path) plus xcind-config --generate-starship emitting a ready-to-paste Starship [custom.xcind] block.
capabilities.why_it_exists: Emergent: users wanted their prompt to show the current workspace/app and a clickable apex URL. Needed a budget-safe trimmed context detection (stops before the expensive jq/Docker/hook block) plus a generator emitting the Starship config block.
capabilities.rationale: A context-aware prompt segment (workspace/app/apex-url, OSC 8 hyperlink, --detect availability) is a genuinely useful, language-neutral capability. Scind's shell-integration spec covers scind-compose + completion but has no prompt-segment concept at all, not even in Future Considerations.
capabilities.target_scind_change: Add a 'scind prompt' command (and a generate path emitting a Starship snippet) to docs/specs/shell-integration.md and docs/reference/cli.md; specify the fast context-only detection path and the --print field / --detect / apex-url semantics.

capabilities.capability: Per-application export introspection: 'ports' / 'urls' / 'exports' subcommands with a [SERVICE] filter and pipe-friendly bare-scalar output (e.g. open $(xcind-application urls web)), all sharing the [SERVICE] [DIR] [--json] shape.
capabilities.why_it_exists: Emergent (ADR-0015 / PR #69): per-app routing state (assigned host ports, computed proxied URLs) was only readable via docker inspect|jq or a raw TSV; tooling/CI had no stable contract. A SERVICE arg prints the bare value so it pipes cleanly.
capabilities.rationale: Scind has workspace-level 'urls', global 'port list', and 'app show', but no per-app command returning a single export's port/URL as a bare scalar for scripting, nor a unified per-app 'exports' view. ADR-0015 itself flags a complementary global view as desirable follow-up.
capabilities.target_scind_change: Add scind app urls/ports (or scind app exports) to docs/reference/cli.md with -q/scalar output for a single service; align the JSON shape with the assignedExports/proxiedExports maps.

capabilities.capability: Unified per-export descriptor merging assigned + proxied exports keyed by export name (type/composeService/containerPort/hostPort|url/tls/apex) in one rich view.
capabilities.why_it_exists: Users think in terms of 'exports' (a service's externally reachable surface), which Scind/Xcind split across two port types. A single name-keyed descriptor merging both types answers 'what is reachable and how' in one call.
capabilities.rationale: Scind models proxied vs assigned as separate concepts (ADR-0007) surfaced through separate commands; there is no single unified per-export descriptor spanning both types. A real ergonomic improvement.
capabilities.target_scind_change: Define a unified export descriptor in docs/specs/port-types.md (or generated-manifest.md) and expose it via scind app show / a new 'exports' view in docs/reference/cli.md.

capabilities.capability: On-demand export of the fully-resolved, flattened compose configuration to stdout or an arbitrary FILE (--generate-docker-compose-configuration[=FILE]), the substrate for the documented Dev Container workflow (bake resolved config into a snapshot devcontainer.json references).
capabilities.why_it_exists: Emergent (ADR-0012): the resolved compose configuration is useful beyond IDEs — devcontainers, CI, debugging, scripting. Scind's 'workspace generate' writes .generated/*.override.yaml deltas into a fixed location; there is no dump-to-stdout / dump-to-arbitrary-path fully-resolved compose document.
capabilities.rationale: Emitting a portable, fully-resolved compose document on demand unlocks external consumers (devcontainers, CI, IDE tooling) and is a distinct capability from writing override deltas. The devcontainer workflow is entirely downstream of it. (Merged: CLI-surface #4 + hooks/generation 'unified --generate-*' + 'devcontainer generation'.) The CLI shape — a subcommand vs a flag on a config command — is Scind's to decide.
capabilities.target_scind_change: Add a resolved/flattened compose-config output (stdout + arbitrary file) to docs/reference/cli.md and docs/specs/generated-manifest.md, and document the Dev Container consumer path that references the snapshot.

capabilities.capability: Standalone POSIX drop-in wrapper generation: --generate-docker-wrapper / --generate-docker-compose-wrapper emit a real executable wrapper script embedding the resolved project name and compose files, for tools that exec a literal docker/docker-compose binary on PATH and cannot call a shell function.
capabilities.why_it_exists: Emergent (JetBrains plugin / external runners that exec docker or docker-compose directly and cannot use a shell function). Scind's context-aware compose is the scind-compose shell function backed by compose-prefix — unreachable by tools that need a real executable.
capabilities.rationale: Tools that invoke docker/docker-compose directly (IDEs, CI runners) cannot use a shell function; a generated wrapper binary is the only mechanism that serves them. The need is language-neutral — Scind faces the same integration surface.
capabilities.target_scind_change: Add a wrapper-script generator (analogous to init-shell) to docs/specs/shell-integration.md / docs/reference/cli.md for tools that require a real docker/docker-compose executable.

capabilities.capability: Build provenance in the version string: XCIND_BUILD_* inject SemVer + build metadata (source channel, short git rev, dirty flag, build date) so bug reports identify exactly what is running across install channels (npm/Nix/Docker/manual).
capabilities.why_it_exists: Emergent: multiple install channels needed version output to disclose provenance so bug reports identify the exact build. Scind's documented version output is a bare 'scind version 0.2.3' with no build metadata.
capabilities.rationale: Disclosing build source/commit/dirty/date is a genuine diagnostic improvement and language-neutral (goreleaser can inject the same fields Xcind reads from env). NOTE: the *mechanism* (env-var injection at build) is Bash-flavored and excluded; the *capability* (provenance in --version) is what promotes.
capabilities.target_scind_change: Extend the Version Information section of docs/reference/cli.md to specify a SemVer build-metadata suffix (+source.rev.dirty.date) populated at build time.

capabilities.capability: Fine-grained workspace registry ops: 'register PATH' (add a cloned-but-never-run workspace with no Docker labels) and 'forget PATH' (drop a single moved/deleted entry by path), beyond init-only registration and prune-all.
capabilities.why_it_exists: Emergent: after cloning/copying a workspace onto a new machine (no containers ever ran, so no Docker labels exist), the user must add it without re-running init; and to drop a single moved/deleted entry by path without pruning everything.
capabilities.rationale: Scind registers only via init, removes only stale entries via prune, and rebuilds from Docker labels via list --rebuild — none handle a cloned-but-never-run workspace or a targeted single-path forget. Explicit register/forget fill a real registry-lifecycle gap.
capabilities.target_scind_change: Add scind workspace register PATH and scind workspace forget PATH to docs/reference/cli.md and docs/specs/state-management.md.

capabilities.capability: Host/container env symmetry via an opt-in host-view env file (XCIND_HOST_ENV_FILE / XCIND_HOST_ENV_MODE): an EXECUTE-phase hook writes a host-flavored dotenv (assigned exports use 127.0.0.1 + published host port; proxied/apex hosts stay the routable proxied hostname) reusing the same discovery seam that feeds container injection, so a committed expression (e.g. DATABASE_URL) resolves identically on the host (mise/direnv) and inside containers. own mode owns the file; block mode rewrites only a marked region.
capabilities.why_it_exists: Scind's service-discovery design specifies only the container view (SCIND_{APP}_{EXPORT}_* injected into containers). Nothing mirrors that surface to host-run processes, so a committed connection string cannot resolve identically on host and in-container. XCIND_HOST_ENV_FILE closes the symmetry.
capabilities.rationale: Self-flagged in ADR-0020 as a deliberate PROMOTE candidate (not a Bash-ism). The concept (host/container env symmetry) and the value-selection logic (which _HOST/_PORT flips per view) are framework-agnostic and absent from Scind's env-injection spec, which addresses only the container view. (Merged: config/env + proxy/export both surfaced it; the proxy agent's ESCALATE was purely a plan-ownership question, resolved to config/env ownership.)
capabilities.target_scind_change: Add a 'host-view env export' capability to docs/specs/environment-variables.md plus an ADR analogous to Scind ADR-0018: opt-in host-flavored dotenv emitting the SCIND_{APP}_{EXPORT}_* set with loopback IP + published host port for assigned exports and the routable proxied hostname for proxied/apex exports; own/block write modes.

capabilities.capability: Application-level env-file handling with an interpolation-vs-injection split: XCIND_COMPOSE_ENV_FILES -> docker compose --env-file (variables for YAML ${VAR} interpolation only; default .env) vs XCIND_APP_ENV_FILES -> a generated overlay adding env_file: to every service (variables available inside running containers; default empty). Each resolved to absolute paths; only existing files included.
capabilities.why_it_exists: Scind has no application-level dotenv concept: its only .env is proxy-level, application.yaml declares compose_files/exported_services but never env files, and Scind states it does not read env vars for its own config. Real apps need both (a) values for compose YAML interpolation and (b) values injected into containers — two different Docker Compose mechanisms.
capabilities.rationale: The distinction between interpolation env (--env-file) and container-injected env (env_file: overlay) is a framework-agnostic Docker Compose modeling insight, not a Bash artifact. The automatic 'inject into every service' behavior and the two-facet split are the transferable learnings; absent by topic from all Scind config schemas.
capabilities.target_scind_change: Add two application.yaml fields (e.g. compose_env_files for --env-file interpolation and app_env_files for env_file: injection into all services) to docs/reference/configuration.md and docs/specs/configuration-schemas.md, with injection generating a compose overlay attaching env_file: to every service.

capabilities.capability: Extensible generation hook lifecycle: user- and built-in-registrable generation participation (custom GENERATE/EXECUTE hooks registered from .xcind.sh) so third parties can inject compose overlays or runtime-prep steps without patching the core pipeline.
capabilities.why_it_exists: Xcind generation is composed of independently-registered hooks so third parties (and future built-ins) can inject overlays without patching the core. Scind's only 'hook' mention (ADR-0014) describes host-gateway following the overlay pattern, not a lifecycle or extension point; its generator is monolithic.
capabilities.rationale: User-extensible generation is a genuine capability whose design concept (third-party overlay generators in a documented lifecycle) is provider-agnostic. But whether Scind (a Go binary with a monolithic generator) should expose a plugin/hook-dir surface is a real product decision, not a mechanical port — Scind cannot source user shell functions, so adopting it means designing a Go plugin/hook contract.
capabilities.escalation_question: Should Scind expose a first-class extensibility surface for generation (a Go plugin API or a hook-directory contract), or keep generation monolithic and closed? This is a product/architecture call.

capabilities.capability: Content-addressed generation cache: SHA-256 over the full pure-input set (compose files + content, env files, .xcind.sh, additional configs/overrides, proxy config, XCIND_TOOLS, naming inputs, instance token, host-gateway config/detection) keys a {sha}/ directory, gated by a .complete marker + per-hook .hook-output-{name} completeness, with atomic rebuild and partial-failure cleanup.
capabilities.why_it_exists: Xcind derives a cache key from every pure input so any change forces a rebuild and identical inputs replay for free; the .complete marker makes aborted builds self-healing. Scind instead compares manifest against configuration to decide regeneration (a coarser, non-content-addressed model) with a --force escape hatch.
capabilities.rationale: Content-addressed keying is more robust than manifest-vs-config comparison (immune to timestamp skew, captures external inputs like detected host-gateway and instance token) — a plausible improvement. But replacing Scind's manifest-comparison staleness model and its fixed .generated/ layout with per-SHA dirs is a substantial architecture change and a human call.
capabilities.escalation_question: Should Scind replace its manifest-vs-config staleness detection with a content-addressed (SHA-of-inputs) cache, including per-SHA generated directories? Architectural decision.

capabilities.capability: Workspaceless / standalone application mode (XCIND_WORKSPACELESS=1): an app with only .xcind.sh and no enclosing workspace runs under full tooling, taking project name {app} (not {workspace}-{app}) and skipping the workspace-internal network entirely.
capabilities.why_it_exists: Xcind lets a single app repo be managed with zero workspace ceremony; naming and networking hooks branch on XCIND_WORKSPACELESS so identity/isolation degrades gracefully to app-only.
capabilities.rationale: Genuine gap: Scind's context-detection treats a missing workspace.yaml as a hard error; its lightest unit is a single-app workspace (path: .) that still requires a workspace.yaml. Whether Scind should add a truly workspaceless mode or keep the workspace.yaml boundary (which it deliberately uses to prevent vendor-package/nested-fixture context hijacking) is a design call.
capabilities.escalation_question: Should Scind support a truly workspaceless single-app mode, or is the mandatory workspace.yaml boundary a deliberate guardrail (context-hijack prevention) worth keeping?

capabilities.capability: Late-bind workspace self-declaration: an application's own committed .xcind.sh may set XCIND_WORKSPACE="name" to join a workspace network/proxy namespace WITHOUT any parent workspace directory (__xcind-late-bind-workspace flips XCIND_WORKSPACELESS 1->0 and derives XCIND_WORKSPACE_ROOT from the app root).
capabilities.why_it_exists: Xcind's config is sourced shell, so an app can declaratively opt itself into a workspace identity, inverting Scind's workspace-owns-apps directionality and enabling workspace networking for a lone repo without a wrapping directory.
capabilities.rationale: Scind has SCIND_WORKSPACE only as a transient context-detection OVERRIDE (equivalent to -w targeting), not a committed app-side declaration of membership. Xcind's app->workspace direction inverts Scind's declarative workspace.yaml-lists-applications ownership model. Genuinely absent and promotable, but adopting it conflicts with Scind's ownership direction — a human call.
capabilities.escalation_question: Should Scind let an application declare its own workspace membership (app->workspace), inverting the workspace.yaml-lists-applications ownership model? Depends on resolution of XA-0021.

capabilities.capability: XCIND_APP_ROOT context-detection escape hatch: an env var that short-circuits the upward .xcind.sh walk and pins the app root directly.
capabilities.why_it_exists: Convenience/testability: lets callers (tests, tooling, non-cwd invocations) bypass the filesystem walk without cd-ing into the app.
capabilities.rationale: Largely covered by Scind's options-based targeting (--app/-a, --workspace/-w; ADR-0011), which already overrides context detection. XCIND_APP_ROOT is an env-var variant of the same idea rather than a new isolation capability. Low priority; flagged only in case an env-based root pin (vs CLI flags) is independently wanted.
capabilities.escalation_question: Is an env-var root-pin (vs the existing --app/-a flag) worth adding to Scind? Likely already covered by ADR-0011 targeting — probably not a real gap.

capabilities.capability: On-demand proxy auto-start: the EXECUTE hook brings up the shared Traefik when an app with a type=proxied export runs (skipped for assigned-only configs), with an XCIND_PROXY_AUTO_START=0 opt-out and a 'config.sh changed; run up to apply' staleness nudge.
capabilities.why_it_exists: Scind auto-starts the proxy on 'workspace up' but documents no first-class opt-out and treats the proxy largely as separately-managed infrastructure. Xcind auto-starts on any app 'up' that declares a proxied export, with an explicit disable knob and a staleness nudge, so developers never hit 'proxy not running'.
capabilities.rationale: A genuine ergonomic capability plus a config opt-out knob absent from Scind, but whether the proxy should auto-start as a side effect of an app run (vs explicit proxy up) is a lifecycle/UX policy decision intersecting Scind's ADR-0010 up/down semantics. (Merged: proxy/export auto-start + config/env XCIND_PROXY_AUTO_START.)
capabilities.escalation_question: Should Scind auto-start the proxy as a side effect of an app-level 'up' (not just workspace up), and expose a first-class opt-out knob? Lifecycle/UX policy call against ADR-0010.

capabilities.capability: Apex reporting mechanics: additive apex_url/apex_host JSON contract fields; consumers prefer the apex URL for the headlining export; live-status host swap; apex opt-out via an empty apex template (ADR-0017).
capabilities.why_it_exists: Scind routes/labels the apex but its specs/behaviors never say the CLI should REPORT the apex URL in preference to the per-export URL, nor define an apex opt-out. Xcind makes apex_url/apex_host additive contract fields, has consumers prefer them, swaps the live-status host, and disables apex when the apex template is empty.
capabilities.rationale: The prefer-apex reporting and additive contract are cleanly promotable, BUT the apex opt-out and 'headlining export' selection are entangled with the positional-vs-primary:true designation conflict that Plan P3 owns (correspondence-map DIVERGED row, ADR 0013 vs 0017). Hand to P3 so apex reporting is not decided twice.
capabilities.escalation_question: HAND TO P3: fold apex reporting-preference and the single-export apex opt-out into the P3 apex-designation decision (Scind 0013 explicit-primary vs Xcind 0017 positional+reporting), rather than promoting reporting in isolation.

capabilities.capability: xcind-proxy init accepts the full proxy config as flags (--tls-mode / --tls-cert-file / --tls-key-file, --dashboard / --dashboard-port, --https-port, --http-port, --image), idempotently merged on re-init.
capabilities.why_it_exists: Emergent convenience: configure TLS mode, custom certs, dashboard, image, and ports at init time (and idempotently merge on re-init) rather than hand-editing config. Scind drives all of these through the declarative proxy.yaml and keeps proxy init minimal (--force/--domain/--path).
capabilities.rationale: The underlying capabilities (configurable ports, per-export TLS, cert cascade) are promoted separately (XA-0004/0005/0008). What remains here is the INTERFACE question — expose them as init flags vs a single declarative proxy.yaml — a product/interface-philosophy call, not obviously right either way.
capabilities.escalation_question: Should Scind surface proxy configuration as 'proxy init' flags in addition to the declarative proxy.yaml, or keep init minimal and config-file-driven?

capabilities.capability: xcind-config doctor [--json]: a generation-pipeline diagnostic dumping GENERATE/assigned-hook registration, which config files were sourced (and order), and how XCIND_PROXY_EXPORTS parsed — i.e. 'explain why routing/generation didn't happen for this app'.
capabilities.why_it_exists: Emergent debugging aid: when a proxied export failed to generate, users had no way to see whether the assigned-hook was registered, which GENERATE hooks ran, which config files were sourced, or how XCIND_PROXY_EXPORTS parsed.
capabilities.rationale: Scind already owns 'doctor' for host/Docker/DNS health. Xcind's doctor mostly surfaces Bash-specific internals (hook registration, config-file sourcing order, TSV path) with no Go analog — but the underlying intent ('explain why routing/generation didn't happen') is general. Needs a human call on scope and naming to avoid colliding with Scind's existing doctor.
capabilities.escalation_question: Should Scind add a generation/routing-explain diagnostic (distinct from its host-health 'doctor'), and under what name? The Bash-internal parts (sourcing order, hook registration) don't transfer; the intent does.

capabilities.capability: Zero-config default compose/env candidate resolution: with an empty .xcind.sh, XCIND_COMPOSE_FILES defaults to 4 standard candidates (compose.yaml/yml, docker-compose.yaml/yml) and XCIND_COMPOSE_ENV_FILES defaults to .env, each existence-filtered, so an app runs out of the box with no explicit file list.
capabilities.why_it_exists: Scind requires application.yaml to list compose_files explicitly per flavor; no auto-detection of conventionally-named compose files and no default env candidate. Xcind lets an app run with an essentially empty config by falling back to Compose's own standard filenames.
capabilities.rationale: A genuine convention-over-configuration convenience absent from Scind, but whether Scind should auto-detect standard compose files (vs its deliberate explicit-declaration model, which pairs with typed flavors) is a product/philosophy call. Scind is convention-based for naming (ADR-0004) yet explicit for compose_files — ambiguous, not a clear PROMOTE or an earned DIVERGENCE.
capabilities.escalation_question: Should Scind auto-detect conventionally-named compose files (compose.yaml, docker-compose.yaml, .env) when none are declared, or keep explicit per-flavor compose_files declaration?

capabilities.capability: XCIND_TOOLS: config-declared host-command -> container-service tool shortcuts (name:service[;use=exec|run][;path=...]) resolved into a JSON tool map, so e.g. 'xcind php' / 'xcind npm' run inside the mapped service.
capabilities.why_it_exists: Xcind lets a project declare named command shortcuts mapping to a container service and execution mode. Scind offers only a generic 'scind exec' — no declarative per-project tool map with exec/run modes and path metadata.
capabilities.rationale: The declarative tool-shortcut map is absent from Scind (which has only generic exec), so it is a real capability — but it is CLI/tool-runner-shaped and its config surface interacts with the settled config-mechanism divergence. Whether Scind should model declarative per-project tool shortcuts (and how, in typed YAML) is a product/interface call.
capabilities.escalation_question: Should Scind add declarative per-project tool shortcuts (host command -> container service + exec/run mode) as a typed application.yaml field, or keep only generic 'exec'?

capabilities.capability: Concern-split generation: eight discrete per-concern overlays (compose.naming/app/app-env/host-gateway/proxy/assigned/workspace/discovery.yaml) merged in a fixed order, vs Scind's single monolithic {app}.override.yaml regenerated atomically.
capabilities.why_it_exists: Each Xcind hook owns exactly one overlay so generation concerns are isolated and (critically) live-state overlays can be cache-invalidated independently of pure ones. Scind emits a single {app}.override.yaml + manifest.yaml regenerated as a unit.
capabilities.rationale: The final merged compose output is equivalent between one override and eight ordered overlays; the split is a consequence of Xcind's per-hook ownership + per-hook caching, not a distinct user-facing capability. The transferable *learning* (separate live-state from pure content) is captured as PROMOTE XA-0001, so it is not lost here.
capabilities.why_scind_should_not_adopt: Scind regenerates its single override file atomically from one generator, so splitting into eight separately-written files would add filesystem and merge-order complexity with no output difference; the split only earns its keep paired with Xcind's per-hook always-run/pure cache distinction, which Scind's atomic-regenerate model does not use.

capabilities.capability: Internal phased pipeline vocabulary: four hook arrays with grammatical-tense phase names (CONFIGURED/RESOLVED are spec'd-but-unimplemented; GENERATE/EXECUTE are live), enforcing 'generation is pure/cacheable, runtime side-effects run every time'.
capabilities.why_it_exists: The GENERATE-vs-EXECUTE split enforces the pure/side-effecting boundary. Scind achieves the same separation implicitly: 'workspace generate' writes override files, 'workspace up' starts networks/containers, with no need to expose the boundary as named phase arrays.
capabilities.rationale: The four-array pipeline is internal architecture structuring Xcind's extensibility model; its value is coupled to the hook-registration capability escalated as XA-0019. If XA-0019 promotes, this phase vocabulary rides along and should be re-evaluated then.
capabilities.why_scind_should_not_adopt: Scind's monolithic Go generator already separates pure generation ('workspace generate') from runtime effects ('workspace up') without surfacing named, individually-registrable phase arrays; adopting the phase vocabulary adds ceremony without a consumer, absent the extensibility surface.

capabilities.capability: Post-hook introspection artifact: cache/{sha}/config.json written AFTER generation hooks run (so it reflects post-hook state: assignedExports/proxiedExports keyed by export name), serving as the machine-readable source of truth for ports/urls/exports introspection (ADR-0015).
capabilities.why_it_exists: Xcind persists resolved routing state as per-app JSON, written after hooks so live values (assignedExports) are captured. Scind covers the same need differently: a workspace-level human-readable manifest.yaml aggregating every app's exported services, hostnames, ports, and environment, plus --json output on commands.
capabilities.rationale: Both tools persist a computed introspection artifact; they differ in shape (Xcind: per-app machine JSON, SHA-keyed, post-hook-ordered; Scind: workspace-level human-readable YAML manifest). Each fits its own topology.
capabilities.why_scind_should_not_adopt: Scind already has an equivalent-or-broader introspection surface (the aggregated workspace manifest); Xcind's per-app SHA-keyed config.json is shaped to its content-addressed cache layout and would not map cleanly onto Scind's single workspace .generated/ directory, so adopting its exact shape would duplicate the manifest rather than add capability.
capabilities.reverse_gap_note: Xcind has NO persisted human-readable aggregated manifest (Scind's manifest.yaml). That is a Scind-ahead item -> P5, not in scope here.

capabilities.capability: Stateless identity/registry model: workspace identity resolved live (source .xcind.sh in a subshell); the only persisted state is a flat path+timestamp TSV (workspaces.tsv). No .generated/state.yaml (flavor state), no computed manifest.yaml, no Docker-label-based registry reconstruction. XCIND_NO_REGISTRY suppresses registry writes entirely.
capabilities.why_it_exists: Xcind keeps identity computed-on-demand to eliminate drift: the registry caches no metadata fields, so a workspace's name/domain/app-count can never disagree with its .xcind.sh, and there is nothing to garbage-collect or reconcile.
capabilities.rationale: Xcind's statelessness is a deliberate scope cut suited to a shell POC, not a learning to backport. The design-assumption flavor (it drops persistence Scind's design relies on) earns the P7 adversarial re-check.
capabilities.why_scind_should_not_adopt: Scind's richer identity persistence (state.yaml for flavor choices, computed manifest.yaml, label-reconstructable workspaces.yaml) is intentional infrastructure for its Go tooling, dashboards, service-discovery consumers, and cache-staleness checks; Scind should NOT downgrade to a metadata-free TSV, which drops capabilities its design relies on.

capabilities.capability: Default proxy domain localhost.scind.io: a registered public subdomain resolving to loopback, yielding a valid trustable wildcard cert with zero /etc/hosts config, vs Scind's .scind.test (RFC 2606, requires local DNS + untrusted cert).
capabilities.why_it_exists: Scind defaults to .scind.test, requiring the operator to configure dnsmasq/hosts and producing an untrusted/self-managed cert. Xcind chose a real registered loopback subdomain so a wildcard *.localhost.scind.io validates on strict stacks and resolves with zero local DNS setup.
capabilities.rationale: The promotable insight — the multi-label constraint — is captured separately as XA-0002, so the learning is not lost. What remains (the specific default value) is Xcind-branded and carries operational trade-offs.
capabilities.why_scind_should_not_adopt: scind.io is an Xcind-owned registration and carries trade-offs Scind may not want (public recursive-resolver leakage of internal hostnames, dependency on monitoring/renewing an external registration, DNS-rebinding-protection failures); Scind's self-contained .scind.test remains a legitimate default.

capabilities.capability: Assigned-port sticky-trust allocation: a sticky-TSV hit is trusted without a bind probe (no self-eviction, no fail-at-startup), with a batched ss/netstat availability snapshot and port-probe-tool degradation telemetry (ss/netstat/dev-tcp fallback for WSL2).
capabilities.why_it_exists: Scind specs sticky assignment, a port_inventory, and net.Listen-based scanning, and FAILS at 'up' when a sticky port is no longer bindable. Xcind trusts a sticky TSV hit without probing because it cannot distinguish 'our own running container' from a foreign listener — probing would self-evict and flap the port — and a truly-stolen port surfaces as a clean docker compose bind error.
capabilities.rationale: The self-vs-foreign ambiguity is real under containerized bind semantics; the batch-probe/telemetry pieces are Bash/WSL2 implementation optimizations. This is a design-assumption divergence (fail-open vs fail-closed) and earns the P7 re-check.
capabilities.why_scind_should_not_adopt: Scind's fail-closed-at-startup model is a valid, arguably safer contract (explicit conflict error + port scan/release remediation); with Go net.Listen probing and a richer port_inventory it should not blindly adopt sticky-trust, which is a trade specific to Xcind's self-vs-foreign ambiguity.

capabilities.capability: xcind-config --check runtime dependency checker (jq / yq / sha256sum|shasum / docker compose) plus a __xcind-maybe-warn-deps nudge on other entrypoints.
capabilities.why_it_exists: Bash-impl need: Xcind shells out to jq/yq/sha256sum at runtime, so it must verify those external binaries exist and nudge when missing.
capabilities.rationale: The Docker/Compose portion overlaps Scind's doctor, but the distinctive part — checking for jq/yq/sha256sum — is meaningful only because the Bash implementation depends on those external tools at runtime.
capabilities.why_scind_should_not_adopt: A statically-compiled Go binary embeds YAML/JSON/hashing and shells out to none of jq/yq/sha256sum, so a runtime check for those dependencies has no meaning in Scind.

capabilities.capability: .xcind.sh trust/security warning printed in status/list/introspection help ('these commands source the .xcind.sh file, which may execute arbitrary shell code; only run against workspaces/apps you control').
capabilities.why_it_exists: Bash-impl consequence: Xcind config lives in .xcind.sh, which is sourced (executed) to read variables, so inspecting a foreign workspace runs its shell code — hence the explicit warning.
capabilities.rationale: The warning exists solely because configuration is executable shell that must be sourced — a direct consequence of the settled config-mechanism divergence (global-context §2a Example A).
capabilities.why_scind_should_not_adopt: Scind configuration is declarative YAML (workspace.yaml/application.yaml) that is parsed, never executed, so inspecting another workspace never runs arbitrary code and no such warning is warranted.

capabilities.capability: --generate-starship --format nix: emit a Nix Home Manager attrset instead of the default TOML Starship block.
capabilities.why_it_exists: Emergent: Nix/Home-Manager users manage their Starship config as a Nix attrset, not a TOML file, so the generator offers a Nix output variant.
capabilities.rationale: An output format tailored to a specific packaging ecosystem, riding on top of the (promotable) Starship-segment capability XA-0010. The prompt-segment concept itself is promoted separately; only the Nix serialization is the divergence.
capabilities.why_scind_should_not_adopt: The Nix Home Manager attrset is a package-ecosystem-specific serialization; if Scind adopts a prompt segment (XA-0010), its output format(s) are its own concern and need not mirror Xcind's Nix variant.

capabilities.capability: xcind-config --preview [-- ARGS]: print the exact 'docker compose' command that would run (project name + -f files + passthrough args) without executing it.
capabilities.why_it_exists: Debug/introspection: show the resolved compose invocation without executing it.
capabilities.rationale: Scind already covers this concept with 'scind compose-prefix' (outputs the resolved docker compose -p ... -f ... prefix for eval by the shell function). Xcind's --preview is the same concept expressed as a config-command flag.
capabilities.why_scind_should_not_adopt: The capability (surfacing the resolved compose command) already exists in Scind as compose-prefix; Xcind placing it as a --preview flag rather than a dedicated command is an arrangement detail driven by Xcind not having a separate compose-prefix command, not a new capability.

capabilities.capability: Shell variable expansion in file patterns (e.g. compose.${APP_ENV}.yaml, .env.$(hostname)) — env-driven selection of which compose/env/config files apply, expanded at resolution time with globbing disabled.
capabilities.why_it_exists: Because .xcind.sh is sourced Bash, env-driven file selection falls out naturally via ${APP_ENV}. Scind achieves the same capability — environment/mode-driven selection of which compose files apply — through its typed flavor system (default_flavor, flavors:{compose_files}).
capabilities.rationale: The capability (env-driven compose-file selection) IS present in Scind as flavors. Variable expansion is intrinsic to the sourced-Bash config mechanism, which global-context §2a already rules a settled DIVERGENCE.
capabilities.why_scind_should_not_adopt: Scind's typed flavor system is the deliberate equivalent for env-driven file selection; adopting shell ${VAR}/$(cmd) expansion in file patterns would reintroduce the exact config-as-sourceable-Bash shape the settled typed-YAML divergence rejects, plus its command-injection surface.

capabilities.capability: Per-file .override sibling auto-derivation and auto-sourcing: for every compose/env/config file Xcind derives a .override variant (compose.yaml->compose.override.yaml, .env->.env.override, .xcind.sh->.xcind.override.sh, docker-bake.hcl->docker-bake.override.hcl) and auto-includes/sources it when present, at both app and workspace level, folding it into the cache SHA.
capabilities.why_it_exists: Xcind gives each base file an unmanaged, user-owned sibling for local layering. Scind meets the same goal — 'layer workspace-specific customization without modifying base, never touched by regeneration' — with a central per-app manual override file at {workspace}/overrides/{app}.yaml.
capabilities.rationale: The 'layer without editing base, preserved across regeneration' capability is present in Scind as its overrides/ directory; Xcind's per-file .override sibling convention is a different implementation shape of the same idea (and the .xcind.override.sh part is pure config-mechanism, already-settled divergence).
capabilities.why_scind_should_not_adopt: Scind already provides the equivalent (manual overrides/{app}.yaml with a preservation guarantee); the per-file sibling convention is an implementation-shape choice tied to Xcind's flat per-app layout and its Bash-sourced config, not a missing capability.

capabilities.capability: XCIND_ADDITIONAL_CONFIG_FILES: declare extra config files to source during resolution (variable-expanded, resolved relative to app or workspace root, with their own .override siblings auto-sourced), enabling composed/layered configuration includes beyond the single .xcind.sh.
capabilities.why_it_exists: Because config is sourced Bash, Xcind supports arbitrary config includes with expansion. Scind composes configuration through its typed layering (proxy.yaml + workspace.yaml + application.yaml/flavors + manual overrides) rather than an include directive.
capabilities.rationale: Config-include/layering is present in Scind via its typed three-schema + flavor + overrides composition. XCIND_ADDITIONAL_CONFIG_FILES is a direct product of the sourced-Bash config mechanism (settled divergence) and inherits its arbitrary-code semantics.
capabilities.why_scind_should_not_adopt: Scind's typed schema layering already composes configuration; adopting a 'source these additional files' include directive would recreate the arbitrary-shell-in-config shape the settled typed-YAML divergence rejects.

capabilities.capability: Two-track documentation (ADR-0014): a user-facing Diátaxis track (docs/) plus a separate engineering LDS track (engineering/) mirroring the design canon, so end-user docs and internal design docs evolve independently without conflating audiences.
capabilities.why_it_exists: Xcind found that a single doc tree conflated two audiences: end users (task/how-to oriented, Diátaxis) and design/maintenance (decision/spec oriented, LDS). ADR-0014 split them into two tracks. Scind keeps a single docs/ LDS tree with no user-facing Diátaxis track.
capabilities.rationale: Human product-call: PROMOTE (overrides the initial classification, which had this as a likely PROCESS/DIVERGENCE per correspondence-map). The two-audience split is a documentation-architecture learning that applies to Scind regardless of implementation language.
capabilities.target_scind_change: Rename Scind's design-canon tree docs/ -> engineering/ (matching Xcind's LDS track), and optionally add a placeholder docs/README.md stating the intent to house a future user-facing Diátaxis track (no real Diátaxis content exists yet in either project).

capabilities.capability: XDG Base Directory Specification compliance with a proper config/state split: config lives under $XDG_CONFIG_HOME (~/.config/xcind), while ephemeral machine-local state (assigned-port TSV, workspace registry, proxy state) lives under $XDG_STATE_HOME (~/.local/state/xcind). Config and state are placed in the XDG-correct roots rather than collapsed into one directory.
capabilities.why_it_exists: Xcind follows the XDG Base Directory Spec, distinguishing declarative config ($XDG_CONFIG_HOME) from ephemeral runtime state ($XDG_STATE_HOME). Scind's env-var spec defaults BOTH SCIND_CONFIG_DIR and SCIND_STATE_DIR to ~/.config/scind, collapsing state under config-home and ignoring $XDG_STATE_HOME/$XDG_DATA_HOME.
capabilities.rationale: Human product-call: PROMOTE. Scind's structure-vs-state separation (ADR-0005) is exactly what XDG encodes at the filesystem level — declarative config is version-controllable/backup-worthy, ephemeral state is not. Collapsing both under $XDG_CONFIG_HOME contradicts that principle and is a portable design fix, not a Bash-ism.
capabilities.target_scind_change: In docs/specs/environment-variables.md / state-management.md, default SCIND_STATE_DIR to $XDG_STATE_HOME/scind (falling back to ~/.local/state/scind) and keep SCIND_CONFIG_DIR under $XDG_CONFIG_HOME/scind; align global state placement with the XDG Base Directory Spec (config vs state vs data).

reverse_gaps.note: Xcind is BEHIND Scind, not ahead — out of P4 scope, hand to P5/P3.
reverse_gaps.capability_scind_has: Scind's host-gateway-resolution.md ('Environment Variable Exposure') mandates (SHOULD) exposing the resolved workstation host as SCIND_HOST_GATEWAY inside containers (Xdebug client_host use case).
reverse_gaps.xcind_state: Xcind's host-gateway hook writes only extra_hosts: host.docker.internal:<value> and never injects the *_HOST_GATEWAY env var (lib/xcind/xcind-host-gateway-lib.bash:150-224).
reverse_gaps.recommendation: ESCALATE to P5/P3: either implement XCIND_HOST_GATEWAY env injection to satisfy the Scind SHOULD, or record a deliberate Xcind divergence. Distinct from the ADR-0018 discovery vars.

reverse_gaps.note: Xcind is BEHIND Scind (dropped a Scind feature) plus a label-naming divergence — out of P4 scope, hand to P5/P3.
reverse_gaps.capability_scind_has: Scind emits per-export/per-port visibility labels (scind.export.{n}.proxy.{proto}.visibility and port.{n}.visibility, public|protected, default protected) so external tools can filter/display services by intent.
reverse_gaps.xcind_state: Xcind emits no visibility labels at all, and its export label schema drops the 'proxy.' segment (xcind.export.{n}.http.url vs scind.export.{n}.proxy.http.url) (lib/xcind/xcind-proxy-lib.bash:465-530).
reverse_gaps.recommendation: ESCALATE to P5/P3: decide whether Xcind should restore visibility labels and whether the 'proxy.'-segment naming difference is intentional or should re-converge.
